coredata.dc:title: Confining metal nanoparticles and nanoclusters in covalent organic frameworks for biosensing and biomedicine

coredata.dc:description: Metal nanoscale particles, primarily including metal nanoparticles (MNPs) and nanoclusters (MNCs), have garnered substantial interests owing to their unique electronic configurations and distinct physicochemical properties. However, practical applications are frequently constrained by their limited stability and aggregation tendency. Covalent organic frameworks (COFs), featuring highly ordered periodic architectures, have emerged as ideal porous matrices for hosting metal nanoparticles. The resulting metal-embedded COFs synthesized through adsorption methods (M/COFs) or in-situ reduction (M@COFs) not only mitigate nanoparticle aggregation and enhance stability but also demonstrate synergistic effects that generate enhanced or novel functionalities, significantly broadening their application potential. This review firstly examines adsorption-based synthesis strategies for M/COFs through physical and chemical approaches. Subsequently, we analyze in-situ reduction methods for M@COFs, categorizing them by reduction pathways: deposition, impregnation-pyrolysis, and “one-step” synthesis. Special attention is given to an emerging pore wall engineering strategy within in-situ reduction approach. The biosensing and biomedical applications of metal-embedded COFs are systematically examined, highlighting their comparative advantages over conventional nanomaterials in sensing and antimicrobial applications. While metal-embedded COFs remain in their developmental infancy and face considerable challenges, the controlled synthesis of multifunctional variants promises transformative potential across biomedical domains.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Metal nanoscale particles, primarily including metal nanoparticles (MNPs) and nanoclusters (MNCs), have garnered substantial interests owing to their unique electronic configurations and distinct physicochemical properties. However, practical applications are frequently constrained by their limited stability and aggregation tendency. Covalent organic frameworks (COFs), featuring highly ordered periodic architectures, have emerged as ideal porous matrices for hosting metal nanoparticles. The resulting metal-embedded COFs synthesized through adsorption methods (M/COFs) or in-situ reduction (M@COFs) not only mitigate nanoparticle aggregation and enhance stability but also demonstrate synergistic effects that generate enhanced or novel functionalities, significantly broadening their application potential. This review firstly examines adsorption-based synthesis strategies for M/COFs through physical and chemical approaches. Subsequently, we analyze in-situ reduction methods for M@COFs, categorizing them by reduction pathways: deposition, impregnation-pyrolysis, and “one-step” synthesis. Special attention is given to an emerging pore wall engineering strategy within in-situ reduction approach. The biosensing and biomedical applications of metal-embedded COFs are systematically examined, highlighting their comparative advantages over conventional nanomaterials in sensing and antimicrobial applications. While metal-embedded COFs remain in their developmental infancy and face considerable challenges, the controlled synthesis of multifunctional variants promises transformative potential across biomedical domains.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Nanoscale metal particles containing a few to few thousands metal atoms are composed of nanoparticles (MNPs, size <100 nm) and nanoclusters (MNCs, size <3 nm), which encompass the bulk-to-molecule transition region (Jin et al., 2016). Nanoscale sizes of metal particles result in confinement for metal free electrons, giving rising to totally different physicochemical properties from their parent bulk materials. MNPs exhibit localized surface plasmon resonances (LSPR) due to the collective oscillation of free electrons under electromagnetic irradiation, which could be applied in biosensing based on surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) detection (Li et al., 2014; Sperling et al., 2008). Particularly, the large absorption to the near-infrared (NIR) window of MNPs also allows the potential applications as therapeutic agents (Arvizo et al., 2012; Chen et al., 2024; Evans et al., 2018). While for MNCs, the discrete electronic energy levels and strong quantum size effects endow MNCs with distinctive physicochemical characteristics, such as intense photoluminescence (PL), significant Stokes shift, and HOMO-LUMO transition (Li et al., 2024a; Maity et al., 2025; Qian et al., 2012). Additionally, the inherent biocompatibility, superior pharmacokinetics, renal clearance, and biodegradation characteristics, endow MNCs with great promise in biolabeling, biosensing, bioimaging, antimicrobial, and targeted cancer therapy applications (Lin et al., 2021; Ndugire et al., 2023; Qian et al., 2022; Xiao et al., 2021; Zheng et al., 2021). For example, an ultrasmall AuNC-based theranostic probe was designed, composing of atomically precise and NIR-II emitting Au44MBA26 NCs conjugated with immune checkpoint inhibitor NLG919 via a singlet oxygen (1O2)-cleavable linker (Au44MBA26-NLG). Upon NIR photoirradiation, the Au44MBA26-NLG not only enables NIR-II photoluminescence imaging of tumors in deep tissues for guiding tumor therapy, but also allows synergistic effects of cancer photothermal therapy (PTT), photodynamic therapy (PDT) and the photogenerated 1O2-induced release of NLG919 for cancer immunotherapy (Yang et al., 2023b). Another example was constructed for an intratumorally administered NIR-II/SWIR fluorescent probe (AuPC) utilizing molecular Au25 cluster with stealth phosphorylcholine coating as a preclinical intratumorally injectable agent, affording homogeneous tumor labeling and imaging-guided resection with rapid renal excretion, high biocompatibility, and safety (Baghdasaryan et al., 2024).

Nevertheless, the high specific surface area of metal nanoscale particles yields poor stability and tendency to aggregate, limiting their further practical applications (Guo et al., 2024). To address inherent limitations of metal nanoscale particles, efforts have been made to integrate metal nanoscale particles into supports with high surface areas (Liu and Corma, 2021; Zheng et al., 2024a), including polymers (Kou et al., 2022; Song et al., 2021), silica (Shu et al., 2022; Zhu et al., 2019), metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) (Casteleiro et al., 2021), and stacked 2D materials (Liu et al., 2024a; Oshima et al., 2015). For example, Pt NCs were integrated with finely tuned ZIF-8 as nanozyme, achieving much enhanced peroxidase (POD)-like activity and reactive oxygen species (ROS) generation capacity. The obtained ZIF-8-pPt could effectively lead to spontaneous apoptosis and bacterial rupture, simultaneously accelerate spontaneous wound healing (Yu et al., 2024). For another example, a novel hybrid architecture was built using MoO2.0N0.5 atomic NCs bonded in nanosheets of N-doped carbon hierarchical hollow microspheres. The obtained MoO2.0N0.5/NC with enhanced electrochemical kinetic could be applied as an anode material for sodium-ion batteries (Pan et al., 2022). However, the surface and pore structure of these solid supports are vague and hard to be controlled at sub-nanometer scale, generating possible aggregation inside pores and uncertainty in property regulation.

Covalent organic frameworks (COFs) are crystalline porous polymers with highly ordered and periodic framework structure, which are constructed by pre-designed building blocks via dynamic covalent connection (Geng et al., 2020a; Liu et al., 2021c; Tan et al., 2023). The COFs exhibit unique advantages including mouldability and maneuverability of structure (Wang et al., 2019a; Zhao et al., 2020), high surface area (Fan et al., 2018; Zhao et al., 2020), low density (Wang et al., 2019a), and fine-tuned physicochemical properties (Albacete et al., 2019; Das and Mandal, 2018), which endow COFs with a variety of potential applications in gas storage and adsorption (Geng et al., 2020b; Konavarapu and Biradha, 2018), molecular separation (Das and Mandal, 2018; Han et al., 2018; Wu et al., 2019), catalysis (Bhunia et al., 2016), and optoelectronics (Ding et al., 2018; Li et al., 2018). Besides, the COFs with photoluminescence tunability have been applied to the fields of biosensing and bioimaging, which include detection of molecular analytes, cell imaging, in vivo imaging, and drug delivery (Guo et al., 2021; Haug et al., 2020; Ma et al., 2022). For example, an activatable COF nanosensitizer (RB@COFs-MnOx-PEG) was constructed with high synergistic efficacy of tumor-specific sonodynamic therapy and ferroptosis (Zhang et al., 2023d). Also, a COF-modified carbon fiber microelectrode (cCFE) exhibited outstanding antibiofouling and antichemical fouling ability, excellent analytical selectivity and sensitivity, which could be applied into the real-time detection of dopamine in the mouse brain (Zhou et al., 2023). Recent studies underscore the potential of COFs as ideal supports due to their ordered structure and uniform pores, which effectively mitigate the growth and aggregation of metal nanoscale particles, and the surface functional groups of COFs could be tailored for anchoring metal ions to achieve high metal loading efficiency (Lu et al., 2017; Xu et al., 2021). The finally obtained metal-embedded COFs composites could integrate respective merits of COFs and metal nanoscale particles, potentially yielding excellent even novel properties (Zhou et al., 2024a). For example, the physicochemical stability of AuNCs under prolonged light illumination could be much improved via being encapsulated into ultrasmall pores of the thiol-functionalized COFs (Deng et al., 2020). Another example of fluorinated COFs showed strongly confinement of PdNCs by strong metal-support interaction (MSI),which could not only improve the stability of PdNCs, but also promote electron transport then to enhance photocatalytic activity of composites (Liu et al., 2023a).

Notably, metal-embedded COF composites exhibit synergistic advantages by combining the porous structures and regular topologies of COFs with the intriguing physicochemical properties and excellent biocompatibility of nanoscale metal particles. These composites present exceptional and even novel properties that hold significant potential for bio-related applications. While there have been reviews on the catalytic applications of metal-embedded COF composites, comprehensive discussions specifically focusing on their bio-related applications remain limited. This review aims to address this gap by summarizing recent advancements in the biosensing and biomedical applications of metal-embedded COF composites. Firstly, the synthetic strategies for metal-embedded COFs are classified and analyzed. These include the adsorption method for producing M/COFs and in-situ reduction techniques for generating M@COFs via various metal reduction pathways, such as deposition, impregnation-pyrolysis, and “one-step” reduction. Additionally, the pore wall engineering strategies associated with in-situ reduction methods are discussed in detail. Subsequently, bio-related applications of metal-embedded COF composites are reviewed. These include their use in biosensing through diverse detection signals, such as colorimetric, fluorescence, electrochemical, SERS, and multi-mode detection systems, as well as in biomedical applications, such as antimicrobial agents. The advantages of metal-embedded COF composites in biosensing and biomedicine, compared to other nanomaterials, are also explored. Finally, this review offers an outlook on the challenges and future directions of metal-embedded COFs, particularly in achieving controllable synthesis and expanding their biological applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The synthetic strategies for metal-embedded COFs composites
ce:section.ce:para: The interactions between metal nanoscale particles and COFs skeletons play a crucial role in aggregation prevention and decomposition resistance. As a result, COF-based materials serving as supports could not only control the incorporation of metal particles but also facilitate control over their size and distribution due to their highly designable as well as stable framework structure, and tailorable physicochemical properties of pore walls. Based on the formation mechanisms of metal-embedded COFs composites, the synthetic strategies could be classified into two main categories: (1) adsorption method to obtain M/COFs: metal nanoscale particles are pre-synthesized and capsulation during the formation of COFs or mixing with prepared COFs through physical adsorption and chemical adsorption; (2) in-situ reduction to obtain M@COFs: metal nanoscale particles are in-situ synthesized within the COFs pores upon reducing environment including different metal reduction pathways: deposition, impregnation-pyrolysis and “one-step” reduction. And the pore wall engineering strategy of in-situ synthesis is paid a special attention on a recent interest, which means the pore wall of COFs is functionalized with anchoring groups as nucleation sites for metal nanoscale particles synthesis (Scheme 1 ).

Of note, the rationale for the naming of M/COFs and M@COFs refers to common expressions in the fields of MNCs-MOF composites, where “/” and “@” are used to distinguish the combination mode and location of metal (M) with COFs. Specifically, “/” indicates that the pre-synthesized metal nanoscale particles are combined with COFs in the form of physical loading or surface modification. The metal nanoscale particles may be distributed on the surface or in the pores of the COFs. “@” indicates that the metal nanoscale particles are encapsulated or embedded within the COFs, forming a core-shell structure or in-situ confinement. The metal nanoscale particles are encapsulated by the COFs framework, usually achieved through in-situ reduction, and possess stronger spatial confinement and stability (Li et al., 2023e; Luo et al., 2018; You et al., 2024).
From a comprehensive analysis of each synthetic strategy as shown in Table 6 we summarize as follows:

The adsorption methods comprising of physical adsorption and chemical adsorption are utilized for the preparation of M/COFs. The physical adsorption has simple synthetic procedure without chemical modification, while may cause low loading efficiency of metal. The chemical adsorption through coordination or covalent interaction could enhance stability of metal nanoscale particles and improve loading efficiency. However, the adsorption methods fail to confine the size of metal nanoscale particles and consequently do not achieve the desired enhancement effect.

The in-situ reduction for M@COFs refers to a process whereby metal nanoscale particles are synthesized subsequent to the formation of the COF supports. The deposition method with simple procedure could tune the particle size by controlling deposition conditions, while the weak interaction between metal precursors and COFs may cause randomly distributed aggregates rather than monodisperse nanoclusters. The impregnation-pyrolysis could realize higher metal loading efficiency and size-confinement effect via uniform dispersion of metal precursors within ordered nanopores of COFs, while the weaknesses include aggregation of metal nanoscale particles and partial framework amorphization of COFs during pyrolysis upon high temperature, and sophisticate procedure requiring precise atmosphere control. As for the “one-step” reduction with advantages like general and wild synthesis, and tunable size of metal nanoscale particles via reductive parameter modulation, the introduction of exogenous ligands or reducing agent may cause post-synthesis purification challenges and pore blockage that may hinder subsequent applications. Of note, as the special case of “one-step” reduction, the pore wall molecular engineering enables exceptional metal loading capacities and size-confinement effect through precisely engineered coordination interfaces between COF skeletons and metal precursors.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Biosensing applications of metal-embedded COFs composites
ce:section.ce:para: As supports for metal nanoscale particles, COFs demonstrate considerable potential for biomedical applications, particularly in the realm of biosensing, owing to their intrinsic advantages, including high sensitivity and selectivity, excellent biocompatibility, remarkable signal amplification capability, and distinctive enzyme-mimicking catalytic activity (Guo et al., 2021; Xu and Huang, 2022; Zhu et al., 2023). For instance, Yu et al. developed a fluorescent thioether-functionalized COF (TTB-COF), which demonstrated significant fluorescence quenching upon interaction with Au ions, attributed to the strong and selective affinity of sulfur atoms with Au. Based on the mechanism, the TTB-COF could realize selective sensing and recovery of trace-level Au in water with a low detection limit of 1.39 μM (Zhou et al., 2018).

Metal-embedded COFs composites combine the synergistic advantages of COFs, such as tunable porosity, moldability, maneuverability, and excellent stability, with the interesting size-dependent electrical, optical, and chemical properties of metal nanoscale particles (Qiao et al., 2021; Si et al., 2022; Zhang and Wang, 2014), which endow metal-embedded COFs composites with widely promising prospects in biosensing (Table 7 ). In addition, comparisons have been drawn between metal-embedded COF composites and other nanomaterials. Notably, metal-embedded COFs can be customized by adjusting the type of metal, the size of the nanoscale metal particles, and the functional groups within the COF skeletons. This design flexibility makes them highly versatile and adaptable for a wide range of application scenarios.

The colorimetric methods for biosensing using metal-embedded COFs typically rely on excellent catalytic performance of metal nanoclusters and nanoparticles. Furthermore, the integration with COFs could endow composites with enhanced catalytic activity, rendering them highly effective for biosensing applications. For example, Lu et al. fabricated AgNPs-embedded COFs (AgCOFs) through “one-step” reduction approach, where AgNPs with an average diameter of within 10 nm and a broad size distribution were generated on COF supports using NaBH4 at ambient temperature (Fig. 6 A and B). The AgCOFs could catalyze the reaction of trisodium citrate-AgNO3 to form AgNPs, following the change of solution color from colorless to yellow (Yao et al., 2022a). The presence of Pb2+ aptamer wrapped on the surface of AgCOF could inhibit catalytic activity, resulting in decreasing absorption signal. As shown in Fig. 6C, with the increasing concentration of Pb2+, which conjugates to Pb2+ aptamer and forms a stable G-quadruplex structure, the catalytic activity of AgCOF is restored, resulting in gradual increasing in the absorption signal accompanied by the deepening yellow color of the solution. This novel “on-off-on” colorimetric analysis platform can be effectively applied for the detection of trace Pb2+ with the detection limit as low as 0.060 nmol/L in water samples.

In 2024, Xiao et al. developed a magnetic and POD-specific COF confined osmium (Os) nanoclusterzyme Fe3O4@COF@Os via “one step” reduction of K2OsCl6 via ascorbic acid (AA) or NaBH4 upon 80 °C, resulting in the formation of Os nanocluster on the Fe3O4@COF skeleton (Fig. 7 A) (Zhou et al., 2024c). As shown TEM images in Fig. 7B, ultrafine OsNCs existed over the COF shell. Subsequently a lateral flow assay (LFA) was established for serum cancer biomarker prostate-specific antigen (PSA) leveraging the superior POD-specific activity and negligible oxidase (OXD) as well as catalase (CAT) mimic activity of Os nanocluster, alongside the superparamagnetism of Fe3O4 to separate targets. Specifically, the Fe3O4@COF@Os was modified with PSA antibody through electrostatic adsorption, taking advantage of the adsorption properties of COFs to obtain PSA antibody-Fe3O4@COF@Os, which could effectively capture the PSA protein in human serum. The Fe3O4@COF@Os/PSA was then separated using a magnet and dropped on a strip for immunochromatography detection. The anti-PSA antibodies immobilized on the T line could capture Fe3O4@COF@Os/PSA and form sandwich complexes. Subsequently, the color amplification solution (TMB + H2O2) is dropped, the enhanced blue signal of T line could be readout visually, and allows for quantitative analysis of PSA concentration achieving a low detection limit of 3.83 pg/mL, while exhibiting negligible background interference and good recyclability (Fig. 7B).

“One step” reduction emerges as the most widely adopted strategy for preparation of metal-embedded COFs owing to its versatility in achieving tailored nanoparticle sizes through precise control of reduction parameters (e.g., reaction time, temperature, or reducing agent type and dosage), while maintaining simplicity and scalability. As the special case of “one-step” reduction, pore-wall engineering has demonstrated significant potential in constructing metal-embedded COFs for catalytic applications (Deng et al., 2020; Guo et al. 2024; Kou et al., 2023). While for bio-related applications of metal-embedded COFs, the researches about pore-wall functionalized COFs for MNCs embedding remain underexplored. Xu et al. fabricated a novel core-shell nanocomposite of prussian blue@Fe-covalent organic framework@Au (PB@Fe-COF@Au) as shown in Fig. 8 A, of which the abundant sulfur-containing groups of aldehyde monomer (Thieno[3,2-b]thiophene-2,5-dicarbox-aldehyde) in COFs construction could effortlessly anchor AuNPs to COF shell via pore-wall molecular engineering (Fig. 8B) (Li et al., 2023b). Introduction of acetylcholine-esterase (AChE) and its substrate triggered an enzymatic cascade reaction producing thiocholine (TCh), which competitively inhibited the peroxidase-like activity of PB@Fe-COF@Au, resulting in attenuated absorbance at 652 nm and visible color fading. Conversely, when chlorpyrifos (a potent AChE inhibitor) was present, it suppressed AChE activity and consequently restored the nanocomposite's catalytic capacity, as evidenced by recovered absorbance intensity and chromogenic response, which could realize a wider linear range of 10–800 ng mL-1 with a relatively lower detection limit of 0.61 ng mL-1 for chlorpyrifos detection (Fig. 8C). Based on this mechanism, the researchers developed a three-dimensional hydrogel-based detection platform, which achieved visual semiquantitative analysis of chlorpyrifos residues through colorimetric readouts, demonstrating advantages in operational simplicity, cost-effectiveness, and instrument-independent applicability for on-site environmental monitoring.

Fluorescence sensing has garnered significant attention in the fields of bio-detection and bio-imaging owing to its exceptional sensitivity and selectivity, as well as its capability for monitoring (Basabe-Desmonts et al., 2007). Wang et al. utilized AuNPs/COF NSs composites by growing small AuNPs with a size of 3.9 ± 0.5 nm (Fig. 9 A) through “one step” reduction upon 80 °C on two-dimensional COF nanosheets (AuNPs/COF NSs) as effective quenchers for the label-free fluorescent AgNCs (Tian et al., 2020). As shown in Fig. 9B, the ssDNA sequences are comprised of probe ssDNA to recognize the target sequence and template DNA for synthesizing the fluorescent AgNCs. The fluorescence of AgNCs could be quenched when the ssDNA probe adopts a nearly flat configuration on the surface of AuNPs/COF NSs via strong non-covalent binding. Whereas in the presence of target DNA, the ssDNA probe hybridizes with its complementary sequence to form double-stranded DNA causing it to detach from the surface of AuNPs/COF NSs, resulting in the recovery of fluorescence. Thus, the multiplexed detection of hepatitis A virus DNA (HAV) and hepatitis B virus DNA (HBV) could be realized using AuNPs/COF NSs with benign selectivity and picomolar-level detection sensitivity.

In 2022, Liu et al. synthesized FeS2 by in-situ reducing FeSO4 at 200 °C for 12 h, and then encapsulated FeS2 in SNW-1 COF (FeS2@SNW-1) as a novel POD-like nanozyme (Fig. 10 A) (He et al., 2022). As shown in Fig. 10B, spherical FeS2 nanoparticles with medium diameter of 17.5 nm were distributed in the SNW-1 template. FeS2@SNW-1 demonstrated the ability of catalyzing H2O2 to produce hydroxyl radicals (•OH), which in turn oxidize chromogenic substrate o-phenylenediamine (OPD) into its oxidized product exhibiting significant fluorescence and electrochemical activity. In the presence of reductive GSH, the oxidation reaction of OPD was suppressed, resulting in fluorescence quenching and a noticeable decrease in current (Fig. 10C). The FeS2@SNW-1 composite demonstrated superior sensing performance GSH detection, achieving low detection limits of 1.61 μM (fluorescence) and 6.23 μM (electrochemical), along with excellent selectivity, satisfactory stability, and reproducible repeatability across multiple measurement cycles. Subsequently, a portable fluorescence test paper was fabricated for quantitative visual detection of GSH in real time.

Electrochemical sensing technology has gained significant attention from researchers due to its advantages, including low detection limits (reaching picomoles), rapid response time, and the low-cost equipment utilized for sensing (Baranwal et al., 2022; Kimmel et al., 2012). Liu et al. synthesized TAPB-TPA-COFs as support materials and integrated them with PtNPs synthesized via “one step” reducing H2PtCl6 upon boiling for 30 min (Hao et al., 2024). The SEM and TEM images (Fig. 11 A–F) showed that the ultrafine PtNPs were loaded in COFs supports due to confined pore size of COFs. The obtained TAPB-TPA-COFs/PtNPs composites were then dropped onto the pretreated glassy carbon electrode (GCE) surface to obtain TAPB-TPA-COFs/PtNPs/GCE, which exhibited excellent conductivity and exceptional electrochemical performance towards ofloxacin (OFX). Thus, the TAPB-TPA-COFs/PtNPs/GCE electrochemical sensor could be used for the fast and sensitive detection of OFX in water with a wide linear range of 9.901 × 10−3-1.406 μM and 2.024–15.19 μM, with a detection limit of 2.184 × 10−3 μM (Fig. 11G).

CL is an electrochemical technique in which the excited state electron products are generated which subsequently return to ground state alongside the emission of photons. And the concentration of the analyte conveniently correlates with the intensity of luminescence. Therefore, CL detection offers the advantage of high sensitivity to trace analysis, rapid response time, low cost and simplicity (Al Mughairy and Al-Lawati, 2020; Yu and Zhao, 2021). In 2023, Cui et al. synthesized Au-COF composites, where AuNPs were in-situ reduced at room temperature on the COF surface without addition of reducing reagents (Lyu et al., 2023). The confinement of N-(aminobutyl)-N-(ethylisoluminol) (ABEI) and Co2+ into Au-COF (termed as CAACo) resulted in over 20-fold enhancement of CL intensity. As illustrated in Fig. 12 , the detection procedure involved the conjugation of Au-COF with Human epidermal growth factor receptor 2-positive (HER2+) antibody to obtain Ab-CAACo. And the cholesterol-PEG-NH2 was modified on carboxyl magnetic beads (MB) to obtain chol-MB. Different concentrations of extracellular vesicles (EVs) were then incubated with chol-MB to form EV/chol-MB, finally Ab-CAACo was incubated with EV/chol-MB to obtain the Ab-CAACo/EV/chol-MB immunocomplex. The HER2-positive EVs concentration was quantitatively determined through CL intensity measurements, exhibiting a detection limit of 2.6 × 105 particles/mL. This approach thereby offers a novel strategy for designing CL-functionalized sensing materials in advanced biosensing platforms.

RRS is based on elastic photon scattering occurring close to, or at photon absorption wavelengths, which can be utilized to characterize the molecular structure, size, shape and other characteristics of substances. Generally, when the nanoparticles dissociate or break apart, it may lead to decreasing of RRS intensity. Conversely, increasing in nanoparticle size or aggregation typically results in enhancing RRS intensity (El-Kurdi and Patra, 2019). Jiang et al. synthesized COF BzBD doped with PdNCs with size of 5 nm (Fig. 13 A) by reducing PdCl2 upon CO to obtain BzBDPd, and then fabricated a sensitive, fast and simple aptamer platform for detection of melamine (ML), based on the specific reaction of the aptamer-ML and the catalytic amplification strategy of BzBDPd on Au@NiP nanosol (Wang et al., 2022a). The BzBDPd demonstrated an obvious catalytic effect in the formation of Au@NiP alloys with a strong resonance RRS peak at 538 nm. However, the addition of aptamer (AptML) that adsorbed on the surface of BzBDPd by electrostatic effect suppressed the formation of Au@NiP (Fig. 13B). Upon the introduction of ML, a more stable ML-Apt complex was formed which then detached from BzBDPd, resulting in the restoration of RRS signal with a low detection limit of 1.96 × 10−4 nmol/L.

SERS standouts among all the readout sensing technologies due to their unique fingerprint spectral profiles and sharp peaks for each individual analyte, which endows them suitable for simultaneous detection of multiple analytes with rapid responsive speeds, excellent sensitivity, and benign selectivity. Metal nanoparticles with nanogaps or nanotips could enhance the scattering intensity of certain molecules by 104–106 times. It has been reported that metal-embedded COFs composites exhibit improved affinity, anti-interference and reproducibility in SERS detection (Cheng et al., 2022; Huang et al., 2024; Li et al., 2024c; Yang et al., 2023c). He et al. synthesized AuNCs-doped nanofiber COF (AuNCs/COF) by “one step” reducing HAuCl4 at 0 °C. The TEM images showed that AuNPs with average size of 7 nm were homogeneous dispersed inside the porous and on the surface of COFs (Fig. 14 C). Then the AuNCs/COF could be utilized as a SERS-active substrate and Au core-SERS label silver shell nanoparticles (Au@label@Ag NPs) as signal tags to construct a novel SERS sensor for sensitive and simultaneous detection of miRNA-21 and miRNA-155 (Luo et al., 2024). As illustrated in Fig. 14D, the addition of miRNA-21 and miRNA-155 leads to the opening of hairpin structures of H1-21 and H1-155, which are linked to AuNCs/COF substrate, resulting in the formation of H1-21/miRNA-21 and H1-155/miRNA-155 intermediates. Subsequently, the exposed stems of H2-21 and H2-155, which are modified onto the surface of SERS tags (Au@4-MBN@Ag and Au@4-MPY@Ag NPs) recognize and bind to form H1-21/Au@4-MBN@Ag@H2-21 and H1-155/Au@4-MPY@Ag@H2-155 hybrids. The released miRNA-21 and miRNA-155 continue to trigger subsequent cycles, promoting further catalytic hairpin assembly (CHA) recycling assemblies, which results in maximum SERS tags anchored on sensor surface for ultra-sensitive dual detection of miRNAs. Based on the double-enhancement mechanism, Au@label@Ag NPs could realize “multiple-in-one” miRNAs detection with ultra-low LOD, satisfactory detection range, reproducibility, specificity, and good recovery in human serum samples.

SPR sensors relying on excitation of surface plasmons, have gained considerable attention for detection of chemical and biological samples. SPR sensors measure changes in the refractive index occurring in the field of an electromagnetic wave supported by the optical structure (Homola, 2008; Zhou et al., 2019). For example, Jiang et al. synthesized a novel palladium-doped COF (TpPaPd) by in-situ reducing PdCl2 upon CO, and in COFs pores. The TpPaPd could catalyze the reduction of Ni(II) to form Ni-P alloys with a SPR absorption peak at 395 nm (Wang et al., 2021b). The introduction of hemin (HM) and aptamers (AptPb), which adsorbed on the surface of TpPaPd could hinder catalytic reaction and decrease SPR absorption peak. While the addition of target (Pb2+) could form G-quadruplex/HM DNAzyme with Apt-Pb2+, that could enhance the catalytic effect of TpPaPd and restore SPR signal (Fig. 15 ). Compared with other sensing systems for Pb2+ determination, TpPaPd exhibited lower detection limit of 0.0004 nmol/L, high sensitivity and good stability. Thus, a simple, rapid and sensitive SPR spectrophotometric method for the detection of Pb2+ was successfully constructed.

Multi-mode/signal sensing incorporates different sensing elements or transducers to detect diverse signals, such as electrical, optical, chemical, thermal, or mechanical signals which allows for the detection of multiple analytes with high sensitivity and selectivity (Han et al., 2024). For example, Chen et al. synthesized AuNPs with broad distribution of 5–15 nm through “one step” reduction of HAuCl4 by NaBH4 (Fig. 16 A and B). And the obtained COF@Au@Fc hybrids were modified with thiol-functionalized single-stranded DNAs (S0) to obtain COF@Au@Fc-S0. Then, the COF@Au@Fc-S0 hybrids were introduced to the QCM Au chip, where the phosphate groups of phosphopeptide (P-peptide) and S0 chelated with Zr4+ ions. Upon the introduction of protein tyrosine phosphatase 1B (PTP1B), the dephosphorylation of the P-peptide triggered the release of COF@Au@Fc-S0 hybrids from the QCM Au chip, followed by increasing in QCM frequency. At the same time, released COF@Au@Fc-S0 hybrids along with methylene blue (MB)-labeled hairpin DNA (S1-MB) on the Au NPs-modified indium-tin oxide (ITO) electrode, caused MB to be positioned further away from the electrode surface while positioned Fc closer, resulting in a reduced oxidation peak current for MB and an elevated peak current for Fc (Fig. 16C). Therefore, a rapid, sensitive, and reliable QCM-EC dual-mode platform was successfully fabricated for monitoring PTP1B activity in accordance to the changes of QCM frequency and the oxidation peak currents of MB as well as Fc with an ultralow detection limit of 0.0002 pM via QCM mode and 0.0034 pM via dual-signaling EC ratiometric method.

Compared with isolated metal nanoscale materials, the integration with COFs to create metal-embedded COFs offers several advantages. First, this integration prevents the aggregation of nanoscale metal particles. Additionally, metal-embedded COFs can be easily separated from reaction solutions via centrifugation, facilitating detection in complex biological samples (Wu et al., 2018). Furthermore, in contrast to graphene or other carbon-based supports, COFs feature a highly ordered pore structure that provides numerous active sites. This enhances molecular adsorption, concentrates target molecules, and improves the detection sensitivity of biosensors. The structural moldability of COFs also allows for precise, on-demand tuning through the design of organic monomers with specific functional sites, thereby enhancing the specific recognition of target molecules (Li et al., 2023a; Shen et al., 2022; Wang et al., 2022c; Yang et al., 2023c; Zhang et al., 2019). In electrochemical biosensing, COFs offer stable electron transmission and signal amplification, effectively reducing background noise and improving detection accuracy (Jin et al., 2024). Notably, compared to quantum dots (QDs), which suffer from low photostability in biological environments (Wegner and Hildebrandt, 2024), COFs exhibit high structural stability. For example, the photostability of AuNCs has been significantly improved when embedded within COF pores (Deng et al., 2020). Compared to metal oxide nanomaterials, nanoscale metal particles in COFs demonstrate unique catalytic properties, making them suitable for fabricating various biosensing platforms (Wang et al., 2021b; Zhou et al., 2024c). Relative to MXenes, metal-embedded COFs show lower cytotoxicity, lower preparation costs, and simple synthesis processes, making them more practical for real-world applications (Sinha et al., 2018).

Polymer nanomaterials, including hydrogel networks (e.g., polyvinyl alcohol) and electroactive conductive polymers (e.g., polypyrrole), have demonstrated potential as porous supports for encapsulating metal nanoscale particles. However, their intrinsic amorphous architecture and limited specific surface area often result in heterogeneous distribution and low loading capacities of metal particles, thereby influencing the stability and reusability as biosensors (Abhishek et al., 2023; Kailasa et al., 2022; Wang et al., 2021d). In contrast, crystalline porous frameworks (e.g., MOFs and COFs) exhibit ordered pore topologies and ultrahigh surface areas, enabling precise spatial confinement of metal particles, thereby achieving uniform dispersion, enhanced loading efficiencies and practical feasibility. While compared with COFs, both of them have similar strengths in biosensing, including good biocompatibility, regular porosity and adjustable pore size. However, the relatively lower intrinsic stability of coordination bonds in MOFs compared to the robust covalent linkages in COFs, coupled with the constrained functionalization of organic ligands imposed by the coordination geometry requirements of metal nodes, may ultimately restrict practical application of MOFs as high-performance supports for metal nanoscale particles (Li et al., 2023e; Xu et al., 2023a).

In short, metal-embedded COF composites stand out as ideal biosensors among other nanomaterials due to their high stability, on-demand customizability, exceptional sensitivity, and excellent biocompatibility (Table 8 ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Biomedicine applications of metal-embedded COFs composites
ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, the development of novel antimicrobial agents has gained significant attention due to the ongoing emergence of bacterial resistance and the growing health concerns associated with infectious diseases. Metal nanoscale particles have emerged as promising antimicrobial agents due to their great potential in disruption of cellular membrane and protein complexes, as well as degradation of cellular key components such as DNA and proteins (Godoy-Gallardo et al., 2021; Maťátková et al., 2022). Additionally, COFs as supports could not only prevent aggregation of metal nanoscale particles, but also promote the diffusion of oxygen and ROS to trigger oxidative stress in bacteria leading to destruction of bacterial cells due to their high porosity (Table 9 ) (Zhao et al., 2023a).

In 2022, Zhang et al. developed a synthetic strategy to synthesize ultrasmall AuNCs by diffusing 1-vinylimidazole (Vim) into the nanopores of COFs to serve both as reducing agent as well as capping ligand with addition of HAuCl4 (Zhang et al., 2022b). The obtained COF/AuNC nanocomposites showed monodisperse and homogeneously distributed AuNCs (1.8 ± 0.4 nm) in COFs pores (Fig. 17 A). Notably, the COF/AuNC nanocomposites exhibited enhanced ROS generation, low dark toxicity and high photokilling efficiency against E. coli under visible light irradiation, which made them as an efficient photosensitizer for antibacterial photodynamic therapy (Fig. 17B).

Another example was COFBDP/CD-S-3-Ag synthesized via NaBH4 reduction of AgNO3 in pores of COFBDP/CD-S-3, where S element of amino monomer (CD-S-3) prevent aggregation and improve stability of AgNPs (Liu et al., 2023b). Moreover, the significant LSPR characteristics of AgNPs enable the conversion of photon energy into thermal energy under near-infrared light, leading to generation of localized extreme temperature that cause bacterial rupture and death. Consequently, COFBDP/CD-S-3-Ag displayed a synergistic effect of PDT and PTT, effectively eliminated Gram-positive bacteria, Gram-negative bacteria, and fungi, which can be utilized as an environmental friendly and broad-spectrum antibacterial material.

Current reported researches on metal-embedded COFs in biomedicine are predominantly concentrated on antibacterial applications. Compared with discrete metal nanoscale materials, the integration with COFs to obtain metal-embedded COFs demonstrates two key merits: (i) The periodic porous architecture of COFs spatially confines metal nanoscale materials in pores, effectively suppressing their aggregation while preserving accessible active sites (Qiu et al., 2025); (ii) Synergistic effects emerge through the integration of intrinsic photoresponsive properties (e.g., photothermal conversion and photocatalytic activity) of COFs with metal nanoscale particles, significantly enhancing bactericidal efficiency under light irradiation (Mohajer et al., 2023; Wang et al., 2024d). Moreover, the programmable functionality of COFs permits precise incorporation of targeting moieties (e.g., bacterial membrane-specific ligands) or stimuli-responsive units (e.g., pH/enzyme-cleavable linkers), enabling smart antimicrobial delivery systems compared with 2D carbon-based materials. Notably, compared with QDs, metal oxide nanomaterials, and MXenes, metal-embedded COFs exhibit significantly reduced cytotoxicity and superior biocompatibility, positioning them as promising candidates for broad-spectrum antibacterial applications (Nematollahi et al., 2023; Zhao et al., 2023b).

Comparing COFs with other porous supports in metal loading for antibacterial applications, polymer materials may be more flexible to process, such as nanofiber membranes made by electrospinning, to increase the contact area and enhance the antibacterial effect (Avcu et al., 2022; Luo et al., 2021). At the same time, the degradation products of polymers are generally safer and suitable for in vivo applications. While disadvantages may include lower loading efficiency, and metal particles are prone to aggregate in the polymer matrix resulting in reduced active sites. In addition, leaching feature of polymeric antibacterial materials, affecting human body and the environment (Luo et al., 2021). Additionally, akin to COFs, metal-embedded MOFs exhibit biomedical potential as antimicrobial agents due to their synergistic antibacterial mechanisms. However, their practical deployment faces challenges, primarily stemming from the intrinsic instability of under physiological conditions and limited ligand functionalization mentioned above.

Collectively, metal-embedded COFs composites exhibit distinctive advantages for biomedical applications, including synergistic antimicrobial mechanisms, controlled release kinetics, favorable biocompatibility profiles, and precisely tailorable framework architectures. These attributes collectively position them as a promising class of stimuli-responsive nanomaterials in advanced therapeutic development (Table 10 ).

In summary, this review proposes a classified discussion on the synthetic methods for the building of metal-embedded COFs that could effectively prevent aggregation of metal nanoscale particles and enhance both stability and performance. Existing strategies for metal-embedded COFs synthesis could be classified into two main categories. One strategy is physical adsorption and chemical adsorption of pre-synthesized metal nanoscale particles into COFs cavities during the formation of COFs or mixing with prepared COFs to obtain M/COFs. The other strategy is in-situ reduction that mixing COFs supports with metal precursor upon reducing environment to obtain M@COFs, which could be categorized to deposition, impregnation-pyrolysis and “one-step” reduction through metal reduction pathways. Recent advances of metal-embedded COFs in biosensing mainly focus on diverse detection signals, including colorimetric, fluorescence, electrochemical sensing, SERS and so on. The advantages of easy separation, structural and functional tunability, as well as high detection sensitivity, make metal-embedded COFs an ideal component for biosensing platforms. Additionally, metal-embedded COFs can be employed in antibacterial applications, leveraging the synergistic effects of the antimicrobial properties of metal nanoparticles and the rich porosity of COFs. Despite the promising structural and functional attributes of metal-embedded COFs, this field remains in its infancy. Significant gaps persist in both the structural diversity and functional versatility of metal-embedded COFs libraries, while their bio-related applications require systematic expansion and refinement. Critical challenges and future opportunities lie in three interrelated dimensions:

The linkage types of COFs fundamentally govern their physicochemical properties, thereby dictating their application scenarios in biosensing and biomedicine. Current researches indicate that imine- and β-ketoenamine-linked COFs dominate bio-related applications owing to benign crystallinity and stability. Conversely, boron-containing COFs demonstrate limited suitability for biological applications due to the inherent hydrolytic instability of boronic ester and boroxine linkages. Three critical directions warrant prioritization in future investigations: Firstly, the establishment of energy-efficient and cost-effective synthetic protocols to bridge the lab-to-industry gap for scalable metal-embedded COFs synthesis; Secondly, theoretical calculation and advanced in-situ characterization techniques should be combined to elucidate the electron transfer mechanism at metal-COFs interface; Thirdly, implementation of machine learning-guided high-throughput computational frameworks to rationally optimize the chemical compatibility between COF pore-wall functionalities and metal precursors, thereby enabling predictive design of coordination environments for enhanced electronic performance.

Significant researches have been conducted about the synthesis and applications of metal-embedded COFs. However, due to complicated pore microenvironment, interactions between ligands with metal precursors in pores are heterogeneous, resulting in disordered metals loading inside COFs frameworks. Furthermore, despite significant improvement in metal loading efficiency by pore wall molecular engineering, a considerable amount of hollow pores still remain as seen from TEM images. To the best of our knowledge, achieving predictable metal loading efficiency and controllable particles distribution still remains a considerable challenge. Moreover, it is crucial to develop a general paradigm at the atomic level to customize the type of metal and size of particles on demand. And novel metal nanoscale particles could be investigated such as nickel, manganese or other metallic oxide nanoparticles to construct a multifunctional material library with catalytic, photoelectric or magnetic properties.

Current research efforts on the biosensing applications of metal-embedded COFs predominantly exploit the catalytic activities or LSPR effects of embedded metallic nanostructures. To advance metal-embedded COFs toward next-generation biomedical applications, future investigations should prioritize the exploration of their underutilized intrinsic properties, such as framework-engineered fluorescence emission and stimuli-responsive behaviors (e.g., pH-, redox-, or enzyme-triggered structural transitions). Concurrently, functional diversification through post-synthetic modification could unlock novel bio-related applications, including targeted drug delivery via host-guest encapsulation. A critical frontier lies in the rational design of biocompatible linkages such as integrating natural molecular interfaces (e.g., peptide- or glycosylation-mimetic motifs) to minimize immunogenicity while enhancing tissue-specific targeting through ligand-receptor recognition mechanisms.
